elements.sent-2411-002: Regular unsupervised domain adaptive person re-identification (ReID) focuses on adapting a model from a source domain to a fixed target domain.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, an adapted ReID model can hardly retain previously-acquired knowledge and generalize to unseen data.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this paper, we propose a Dual-level Joint Adaptation and Anti-forgetting (DJAA) framework, which incrementally adapts a model to new domains without forgetting source domain and each adapted target domain.
elements.sent-2411-005: We explore the possibility of using prototype and instance-level consistency to mitigate the forgetting during the adaptation.
elements.sent-2411-006: Specifically, we store a small number of representative image samples and corresponding cluster prototypes in a memory buffer, which is updated at each adaptation step.
elements.sent-2411-007: With the buffered images and prototypes, we regularize the image-to-image similarity and image-to-prototype similarity to rehearse old knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-008: After the multi-step adaptation, the model is tested on all seen domains and several unseen domains to validate the generalization ability of our method.
elements.sent-2411-009: Extensive experiments demonstrate that our proposed method significantly improves the anti-forgetting, generalization and backward-compatible ability of an unsupervised person ReID model.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.95\linewidth]{IMG/figure17.pdf} \caption{ReID feature space comparison~\cite{vandermaaten08a} of regular unsupervised ReID method ICE~\cite{Chen_2021_ICE} and unsupervised lifelong ReID method DJAA on three scenarios. 1) Seen domain non-forgetting ability: DJAA preserves previously acquired knowledge, which reduces the gap in the feature space between seen domains. 2) Unseen domain generalization ability: DJAA accumulates domain-shared features that reduce the gap between unseen domains. 3) Backward-compatible ability: With DJAA, updated query (Query\_new) and gallery (Gallery\_new) representations remain in the same feature space with the previously extracted representations (Query\_old, Gallery\_old). Query\_old and Gallery\_old are Market1501 representations extracted after one adaptation step, while Query\_new and Gallery\_new are Market1501 representations extracted after three adaptation steps. } \label{fig:figure1}
elements.sent-2411-010: Although significant improvement has been witnessed in both supervised~\cite{zheng2019joint,He_2021_ICCV and unsupervised domain adaptive~\cite{zhong2020learning,Chen_2021_ICE person ReID, traditional methods only consider the performance of a single fixed target domain.}}
elements.sent-2411-011: In the single target domain scenario, people usually assume that all training data are available before training a ReID model.
elements.sent-2411-012: However, a real-world video monitoring system can record new data every day and from new locations, when new cameras are installed into an existing system.
elements.sent-2411-013: When a model needs to be frequently updated with new data, regular unsupervised person ReID methods can lead to three problems: 1) Once adapted to a new domain, a model is prone to lose the acquired knowledge of previous seen domains.
elements.sent-2411-014: Firstly, as the weather and season are repetitive, losing previous knowledge usually results in a less robust ReID system.
elements.sent-2411-015: To tackle the forgetting problem, lifelong person ReID~\cite{pu_cvpr2021,Wu2021GeneralisingWF has been recently proposed to incrementally accumulate domain knowledge from several seen datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-016: Lifelong person ReID is closely related to incremental (or continuous) learning~\cite{van2022three .}
elements.sent-2411-017: There are three fundamental scenarios for incremental learning, including class-incremental learning, domain-incremental learning, and task-incremental learning.
elements.sent-2411-018: Facing non-stationary streams of data, lifelong person ReID is supposed to simultaneously learn incrementally added new classes and new domain knowledge, which can be defined as a joint class-incremental and domain-incremental learning task.
elements.sent-2411-019: However, previous supervised lifelong person ReID~\cite{pu_cvpr2021,Wu2021GeneralisingWF relies on human annotations for cross-domain fine-tuning.}
elements.sent-2411-020: Replacing supervised cross-domain fine-tuning with unsupervised domain adaptation can substantially enhance the flexibility of a lifelong person ReID algorithm in real-world deployments.
elements.sent-2411-021: Secondly, to enhance the model generalization ability on unseen domain, domain generalization methods~\cite{Song2019GeneralizablePR jointly train a model on several domains to learn domain-shared features.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Regular domain generalization requires that several large-scale domain data are available before training a model.
elements.sent-2411-023: Lifelong person ReID can be an alternative for learning a generalizable model, which does not require collecting all data in advance.
elements.sent-2411-024: By regularizing the model consistency between old and new domains, we gradually accumulate domain-shared information into a unified model.
elements.sent-2411-025: With domain-shared features, an incrementally trained model~\cite{Wu2021GeneralisingWF has proven to be generalizable on unseen domains.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Thirdly, after a domain adaptation step, an updated person ReID model also faces backward-compatiblility~\cite{shen2020towards,wan2022continual problem.}
elements.sent-2411-027: To obtain optimal performance on new data, a lifelong adaptive person ReID system needs to be frequently updated.
elements.sent-2411-028: During the lifelong adaptation, feature representations of previous gallery images need to be re-extracted to maintain a consistent feature space for the pairwise distance calculation.
elements.sent-2411-029: However, re-extracting features after each adaptation step can be time-consuming.
elements.sent-2411-030: An optimal setting for lifelong person ReID system is that an updated model only extracts feature representations from queries and incoming gallery images, while keeping previous gallery image representations unchanged.
elements.sent-2411-031: How to improve the feature consistency between a model and its updated versions remains a key problem for building an efficient system, which is neglected by previous lifelong person ReID methods.
elements.sent-2411-032: We show that anti-forgetting and backward-compatible ability can be unified into the same question for lifelong person ReID, \ie, preserving the representation similarity relationship.
elements.sent-2411-033: In this paper, we propose an unsupervised lifelong person ReID method to simultaneously address the forgetting, generalization ability, and backward-compatibility problems.
elements.sent-2411-034: Unsupervised lifelong person ReID is a challenging yet practical problem.
elements.sent-2411-035: Compared with generic lifelong domain adaptation~\cite{tang2021gradient,rostami2021lifelong , unsupervised lifelong person ReID faces more technical problems.}
elements.sent-2411-036: On the one hand, lifelong domain adaptation usually considers fixed classes across different styles, while unsupervised lifelong person ReID has to deal with non-overlapping identities across domains.
elements.sent-2411-037: On the other hand, unsupervised lifelong ReID aims at learning fine-grained human features, which are usually more sensitive to domain changes than generic object features.
elements.sent-2411-038: Our objective is to train a robust person ReID model that is discriminative to each seen domain while being generalizable to unseen domains.
elements.sent-2411-039: Inter-instance Contrastive Encoding (ICE)~\cite{Chen_2021_ICE formulates image-to-prototype and image-to-image contrastive learning to enhance unsupervised representation learning for person ReID.}
elements.sent-2411-040: The proposed dual-level contrastive learning has proven to be effective in enhancing intra-cluster compactness and inter-cluster separability.
elements.sent-2411-041: ICE shows remarkable performance in unsupervised person ReID.
elements.sent-2411-042: However, when adapted to a new domain, ICE rapidly loses previously acquired knowledge from the source domain.
elements.sent-2411-043: In this paper, we extend ICE to unsupervised lifelong person ReID to enhance the anti-forgetting, generalization and backward-compatible ability during the multi-step adaptation, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:figure1 .}
elements.sent-2411-044: We incorporate pseudo-label based contrastive learning and rehearsal-based incremental learning into a Dual-level Joint Adaptation and Anti-forgetting (DJAA) framework, which enhances anti-forgetting, generalization and backward-compatible ability at the same time.
elements.sent-2411-045: Our proposed DJAA consists of an adaptation module and a rehearsal module.
elements.sent-2411-046: In the adaptation module, we first use a clustering algorithm to assign pseudo labels to unlabeled data.
elements.sent-2411-047: Based on the pseudo labels, we use an image-to-prototype contrastive loss to make images with the same pseudo-label converge to a unique cluster prototype.
elements.sent-2411-048: We also use an image-to-image contrastive loss to further reduce the distance between hard positives.
elements.sent-2411-049: In the rehearsal module, a small number of old domain samples and the corresponding cluster prototypes are selected and stored in a long-term memory buffer.
elements.sent-2411-050: While adapting a model to a new domain, regularizing the image-to-image and image-to-prototype similarity relationship helps to prevent forgetting previous knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-051: Given a frozen old domain model and a trainable model, we set a consistency regularization condition: the image-to-image and image-to-prototype similarity calculated by the frozen old domain model and the current domain model should be consistent during the adaptation.
elements.sent-2411-052: Based on this condition, we regularize the representation similarity relationship with both image-level and cluster-level similarity between the frozen and the trainable models, so that the trainable current domain model can be updated in a way that suits old knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-053: The main contributions of our work are four folds.
elements.sent-2411-054: Our study shows that, those three challenges could be jointly addressed in a Dual-level Joint Adaptation and Anti-forgetting (DJAA) framework.
elements.sent-2411-055: To the best of our knowledge, this is an original person ReID work addressing those three challenges within a unified framework.
elements.sent-2411-056: By introducing data augmentation and domain gap perturbations, we regularize the representation relationship at both cluster and instance levels.
elements.sent-2411-057: DJAA shows remarkable non-forgetting, generalization, and backward-compatible ability on mainstream person ReID datasets.

elements.sent-2411-058: Depending on the number of training/test domains and availability of human annotation, recent person ReID research is conducted under different settings, as shown in Table~\ref{tab:ReID settings .}
elements.sent-2411-059: As the most studied setting, supervised person ReID~\cite{He_2021_ICCV,lee2022negative,zhou2023adaptive,zhang2023pha has shown impressive performance on large-scale datasets thanks to deep neural networks and human annotation.}
elements.sent-2411-060: However, as a fine-grained retrieval task, a ReID model trained on one domain can hardly generalize to other domains.
elements.sent-2411-061: Unsupervised domain adaptation methods~\cite{liu2019adaptive,zheng2021exploiting,zheng2021group,ge2020self,Chen_2021_ICE,cho2022part are proposed to adjust a ReID model to a target domain with unlabeled target domain images.}
elements.sent-2411-062: To maximize the model generalization ability on unseen data, domain generalization ReID~\cite{Song2019GeneralizablePR,Jin_2020_CVPR,dai2021generalizable,zhang2022adaptive,jiao2022dynamically,xu2022mimic is proposed to jointly train multiple labeled domains, in order to learn a generalizable model that can extract domain-invariant features.}
elements.sent-2411-063: The above-mentioned settings simply assume that all training data is available before training.
elements.sent-2411-064: However, in most real-world cases, it is hard to prepare enough diversified data to directly train a generalizable model.
elements.sent-2411-065: Instead, new domain data can be recorded when time and season change or a new camera is installed.
elements.sent-2411-066: Supervised lifelong person ReID~\cite{pu_cvpr2021,Wu2021GeneralisingWF,lu2022augmented,ge2022lifelong,yu2023lifelong is thus proposed to learn incrementally added data without forgetting previous knowledge.}
elements.sent-2411-067: LSTKC~\cite{xu2024lstkc introduces a relation matrix-based erroneous knowledge filtering and rectification mechanism to distill correct knowledge for supervised lifelong person ReID.}
elements.sent-2411-068: However, continuously annotating new domains can be a cumbersome task for ReID system administrators.
elements.sent-2411-069: CLUDA~\cite{huang2022lifelong combines meta learning and knowledge distillation to address the forgetting problem for Unsupervised lifelong person ReID.}
elements.sent-2411-070: However, LSTKC~\cite{xu2024lstkc and CLUDA~\cite{huang2022lifelong have not considered the backward-compatible problem for lifelong person ReID.}}
elements.sent-2411-071: In addition, LSTKC and CLUDA neglect informative cluster prototypes that help to regularize the similarity relationship during adaptation.
elements.sent-2411-072: The main idea of contrastive learning is to maximize the representation similarity between a positive pair composed of differently augmented views of a same image, so that a model can be invariant to view differences.
elements.sent-2411-073: While attracting a positive pair, some contrastive methods also consider other images as negatives and push away negatives stored in a memory bank~\cite{Wu2018UnsupervisedFL,He_2020_CVPR or in a large mini-batch~\cite{chen2020simple .}}
elements.sent-2411-074: Contrastive methods show great performance in unsupervised representation learning, which makes it the main approach in unsupervised person ReID.
elements.sent-2411-075: Based on clustering generated pseudo-labels, state-of-the-art unsupervised person ReID methods build positive pairs with cluster centroids~\cite{ge2020self , camera-aware centroids~\cite{Wang2021camawareproxies and generated positive views~\cite{Chen_2021_CVPR,chen2022learning .}}}
elements.sent-2411-076: ICE~\cite{Chen_2021_ICE combines image-to-prototype and image-to-image contrastive losses to reach the maximal agreement between positive images and cluster prototypes.}
elements.sent-2411-077: However, these contrastive methods only consider single-domain view agreements, which suffer from catastrophic forgetting in multi-domain learning.
elements.sent-2411-078: Incremental (also called continuous or lifelong) learning aims at learning new classes, domains or tasks without forgetting previously acquired knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-079: Previous methods can be roughly categorized into three directions, \ie, architecture-based, regularization-based and rehearsal-based methods.
elements.sent-2411-080: Architecture-based methods combine task-specific parameters to build a whole network.
elements.sent-2411-081: These methods progressively extend network structure when new tasks are added into an existing model~\cite{rusu2016progressive, yoon2018lifelong, mallya2018packnet .}
elements.sent-2411-082: Regularization-based methods consist in regularizing model updates on new data in a way that does not contradict the old knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-083: A common approach is to freeze the old model as a teacher for previous knowledge distillation~\cite{Li2018LearningWF,douillard2021plop,shang2023incrementer .}
elements.sent-2411-084: Rehearsal-based (also called recall or replay) methods address the forgetting problem by storing a small number of old image samples~\cite{Rebuffi2017iCaRLIC,Castro2018End,cha2021co2l,luo2023class or a generative model~\cite{choi2021dual,van2020brain .}}
elements.sent-2411-085: The stored old data or generated data are used to remind the model of previous knowledge during incremental training.
elements.sent-2411-086: In addition to the above-mentioned supervised incremental methods, several attempts have been made in unsupervised lifelong adaptation, such as setting gradient regularization~\cite{tang2021gradient in contrastive learning and consolidating the internal distribution~\cite{rostami2021lifelong .}}
elements.sent-2411-087: CoTTA~\cite{wang2022continual proposes to use weight-averaged and augmentation-averaged pseudo-labels for test-time adaptation.}
elements.sent-2411-088: Differently, our method is designed for lifelong domain adaptation, where we regularize both cluster and instance-level relationship with data augmentation and domain gap perturbations in the training.
elements.sent-2411-089: Moreover, general lifelong adaptation~\cite{tang2021gradient,rostami2021lifelong,wang2022continual has identical classes across different domains, which is not suitable for lifelong ReID that has to learn fine-grained identity representations from non-overlapping classes across domains.}
elements.sent-2411-090: Backward-compatible learning aims at enhancing the backward consistency of feature representations, so that previously extracted representations can be comparable with newly extracted representations in retrieval.
elements.sent-2411-091: BCT~\cite{shen2020towards employs a cross-entropy distillation loss between old and new classifiers to constrain new representations.}
elements.sent-2411-092: Neighborhood Consensus Contrastive Learning (NCCL)~\cite{wu2022neighborhood proposes a neighborhood consensus supervised contrastive learning method to constrain new representations at the sub-cluster level.}
elements.sent-2411-093: AdvBCT~\cite{pan2023boundary uses adversarial learning to minimize the distributional distance between old and new encoders.}
elements.sent-2411-094: However, the above-mentioned backward-compatible learning usually focuses on single-step learning models.
elements.sent-2411-095: In a long session learning scenario, more adaptation steps make it easier to lose knowledge of beginning steps.
elements.sent-2411-096: To handle the multi-step learning scenario, Wan \etal ~\cite{wan2022continual introduce a continual learner for visual search (CVS), which effectively improves the backward compatibility in the class-incremental task.}
elements.sent-2411-097: CVS only considers class-incremental setting within a single domain, which is sub-optimal for the domain-incremental scenario.
elements.sent-2411-098: Oh \etal~\cite{oh2024lifelong propose a part-assisted knowledge consolidation method that leverages both local and global features to enhance the backward compatibility in lifelong person ReID.}
elements.sent-2411-099: Instead of using local features, our framework leverages informative cluster prototypes and instances to enhance the backward compatibility during domain-incremental learning.
elements.sent-2411-100: In this paper, we explore the possibility of jointly addressing the forgetting, generalization and backward-compatible problems existing in regular unsupervised person ReID methods.
elements.sent-2411-101: Although our method is based on contrastive learning, incremental learning and backward-compatible learning, there are major differences between our method and previous methods.
elements.sent-2411-102: Compared with previous contrastive learning that mainly considers single-domain performance, we propose to further reach maximal image-to-prototype and image-to-image similarity consistency across different domains for anti-forgetting.
elements.sent-2411-103: Compared with previous lifelong adaptation methods, our proposed unsupervised lifelong ReID method is able to accumulate fine-grained identity information from non-overlapping classes in a domain-incremental scenario.
elements.sent-2411-104: Moreover, our method uses a dual-level relationship regularization along with contrastive learning to better mitigate the forgetting problem.
elements.sent-2411-105: Previous backward-compatible learning methods, such as CVS~\cite{wan2022continual , only consider class-incremental settings within a single domain, which is sub-optimal for the domain-incremental scenario.}
elements.sent-2411-106: As incrementally added person ReID domains bring in both class and domain gaps, it is more difficult to retain backward consistency in lifelong ReID.
elements.sent-2411-107: Our proposed method regularizes the representation consistency at both cluster and instance levels, which enhances the backward compatibility during domain-incremental learning.

elements.sent-2411-119: We present a Dual-level Joint Adaptation and Anti-forgetting (DJAA) method for unsupervised lifelong person ReID.
elements.sent-2411-120: The general architecture of DJAA is illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:figure2 (a).}
elements.sent-2411-121: To mitigate the forgetting, we build a hybrid memory buffer that stores a small number of informative images and corresponding cluster prototypes for rehearsing old knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-122: The proposed DJAA consists of an adaptation module and a rehearsal module, which work collaboratively to achieve the unsupervised lifelong adaptation without forgetting.
elements.sent-2411-123: For step $s$, we jointly train new samples on domain $D_{s}$ and old samples stored in the hybrid memory buffer to adapt the encoder $\theta_{s-1}$ to become the encoder $\theta_{s}$.
elements.sent-2411-124: The Adaptation Module of DJAA is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:figure2 (b).}
elements.sent-2411-125: We use the pseudo label based contrastive learning method ICE~\cite{Chen_2021_ICE as a baseline, which leverages both an online encoder and a momentum encoder.}
elements.sent-2411-126: In DJAA, the momentum encoder serves as a knowledge collector that gradually accumulates knowledge of each seen domain.
elements.sent-2411-127: During the step $s$, the momentum encoder (weights noted as $\theta_m$) accumulates new knowledge with exponential moving averaged weights of the online encoder (weights noted as $\theta_{}$): \begin{equation}\theta_m^t =\alpha\theta_m^{t-1}+(1-\alpha)\theta^t_{}, \label{equ:ema}\end{equation} where the hyper-parameter $\alpha$ controls the speed of the knowledge accumulation.
elements.sent-2411-128: We extract all image representations with the stable momentum encoder and generate corresponding pseudo labels with a density-based clustering algorithm DBSCAN~\cite{Ester1996ADA .}
elements.sent-2411-129: Based on the clustered pseudo labels, we build cluster prototypes for prototype contrastive adaptation loss $\mathcal{L _{pa}$ and image contrastive adaptation loss $\mathcal{L _{ia}$ (described in Section \ref{sec:Adaptation Module ) on the new domain $D_{s}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-130: The Rehearsal Module of DJAA is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:figure2 (c).}
elements.sent-2411-131: Before adaptation, we freeze the momentum encoder from the last step $\theta_{s-1}$ as an old knowledge expert model.
elements.sent-2411-132: We set the consistency regularization condition: the image-to-image and image-to-prototype similarity encoded by the frozen old domain model and the trainable model should be consistent during the adaptation.
elements.sent-2411-133: Based on the condition, we set an image-to-prototype similarity consistency loss $\mathcal{L _{ps}$ and an image-to-image similarity consistency loss $\mathcal{L _{is}$ (described in Section~\ref{sec:Rehearsal Module ) to regularize the model updates during the lifelong adaptation.}}}
elements.sent-2411-134: To train our proposed framework, we combine the above-mentioned four losses into an overall unsupervised lifelong loss: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{overall} = \mathcal{L}_{pa}+\lambda_{ia}\mathcal{L}_{ia}+\lambda_{ps}\mathcal{L}_{ps} + \lambda_{is}\mathcal{L}_{is}. \label{equ:overall}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-135: Our adaptation module contains a prototype-level contrastive adaptation loss and an instance-level contrastive adaptation loss.
elements.sent-2411-136: Combining these two adaptation losses permits adapting a model to a new target domain, while reducing intra-cluster variance for better knowledge accumulation.
elements.sent-2411-137: Given an encoded query $q$ and a set of encoded samples $K$ = \{$k_0$, $k_1$, $k_2$, ...\}, we use $k_{+}$ to denote the positive match of the query $q$.
elements.sent-2411-138: We define a softmax cosine similarity function: \begin{equation}S(q,K,\tau) = \frac{\exp{(q \cdot k_{+}/\tau)}}{\sum\nolimits_{i=1}^{K}\exp{(q \cdot k_{i}/\tau)}}, \label{equa:softmax cosine}\end{equation} where $q \cdot k$ is the cosine similarity between $q$ and $k$.
elements.sent-2411-139: Inside an unsupervised lifelong ReID pipeline, our model incrementally learns new knowledge on each domain.
elements.sent-2411-140: For step $s$, $D_s=\{x_{1 , ..., x_{n}\}$ where $n$ is the number of current domain unlabeled images.}
elements.sent-2411-141: We first use the momentum encoder $\theta_{m}$ to extract image representations and calculate re-ranking~\cite{zhong2017re based similarity between each image pair.}
elements.sent-2411-142: Based on the pair-wise similarity, the DBSCAN clustering algorithm is utilized to assign pseudo labels $\{y_{1 , ..., y_{n}\}$ to unlabeled images $\{x_{1 , ..., x_{n}\}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-143: Given a current domain image $x_{i}$, $f(x_{i ^{ |\theta)$ and $f(x_{i ^{ |\theta_m)$ denote respectively the online and the momentum representations.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-144: The prototype of a cluster $j$ is defined as the averaged momentum representations of all the samples with a same pseudo-label $y_{j}$: \begin{equation}p^{}_{j} = \frac{1}{n_{j}}\sum_{x_{i}^{} \in y_{j}} f(x_{i}^{}|\theta_m), \label{equa:cluster centroid}\end{equation} where $n_{j}$ is the number of images in the cluster $j$.
elements.sent-2411-145: We use $P$ to denote all the cluster prototypes in the current domain.
elements.sent-2411-146: A cluster prototype adaptation loss maximizes the similarity between a sample $x_{i}$ and the positive prototype in $P$, which can be defined as: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{pa} = -\log S(f(x_{i}^{}|\theta), P, \tau_{pa}) \label{equa:cluster contrast}\end{equation} where $\tau_{pa}$ is a temperature hyper-parameter.
elements.sent-2411-147: The prototype-level adaptation loss $\mathcal{L _{pa}$ makes samples from the same cluster converge to a common prototype and push them away from other clusters.}
elements.sent-2411-148: However, images belonging to the same class can be easily affected by noisy factors, such as illumination and view-point, leading to high intra-class variance.
elements.sent-2411-149: When the adaptation module is only trained with $\mathcal{L _{pa}$, all the image samples converge at a same pace.}
elements.sent-2411-150: Consequently, representations of hard positive samples remain far away after optimization.
elements.sent-2411-151: We use an identity-aware sampler to construct mini-batches.

elements.sent-2411-162: Person ReID is a cross-camera image retrieval task, where the individual camera style is the main factor that causes intra-cluster variance.
elements.sent-2411-163: As shown in ICE~\cite{Chen_2021_ICE , using camera labels to reduce camera style variance can further mitigate the noise during the knowledge accumulation.}
elements.sent-2411-164: A camera proxy $p_{jk}$ is defined as the averaged momentum representations of all the instances belonging to the cluster $j$ captured by camera $c_k$: \begin{equation}p^{}_{jk} = \frac{1}{n_{jk}}\sum_{x_{i} \in y_{j} \cap x_{i} \in c_k}^{} f(x_{i}^{}|\theta_m), \label{equa:center_c}\end{equation} where $n_{jk}$ is the number of instances belonging to the cluster $j$ captured by camera $c_k$.
elements.sent-2411-165: We form a set of camera prototypes $P_{cam}$, which combines one positive cross-camera prototype and $n_{neg}$ nearest negative prototypes.
elements.sent-2411-166: A camera contrastive loss is the softmax log loss that minimizes the distance between an anchor $f(x_{i ^{ |\theta)$ and each of positive cross-camera prototypes: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{cam} = -\frac{1}{|\mathcal{P}|} \sum_{x_{i}\in y_{j} \cap x_{i} \notin c_k} \log S(f(x_{i}^{}|\theta), P_{cam}, \tau_{c}), \label{equa:cameraloss}\end{equation} where $\tau_{c}$ is a cross-camera temperature hyper-parameter, which is set to 0.07 following \cite{Chen_2021_ICE .}}}

elements.sent-2411-201: We store a small number of informative samples and cluster prototype representations in a hybrid memory buffer, which is updated at the end of each step.

elements.sent-2411-204: Suppose that $|P|$ is the number of current domain clusters and $|P^{o |$ is the number of buffered cluster prototypes, we update the memory buffer with $n_{new}$ samples from the new domain and $n_{old}$ samples from the memory buffer: \begin{equation}n_{new} = \frac{|P|}{|P^{o}|+|P|} \times n_{mem}, \label{equa:update number new}\end{equation} \begin{equation}n_{old} = \frac{|P^{o}|}{|P^{o}|+|P|} \times n_{mem}. \label{equa:update number old}\end{equation} Previous methods~\cite{huang2022lifelong,ge2022lifelong usually store several samples from randomly selected classes, which is sub-optimal for our proposed image-to-prototype similarity consistency loss.}}
elements.sent-2411-205: We propose a simple yet effective clustering-guided selection algorithm to select informative samples.
elements.sent-2411-206: As the cluster prototype is defined as the averaged representation, the prototype can cover more sample information if a cluster contains more samples.
elements.sent-2411-207: We thus use the number of samples to rank each cluster and preferentially select clusters that contain more samples.
elements.sent-2411-208: Once a cluster is selected, we proceed to calculate the cosine similarity between the cluster prototype and cluster samples.
elements.sent-2411-209: Under unsupervised setting, we argue that the sample with the highest similarity score is the most credible sample belonging to the cluster.
elements.sent-2411-210: To mitigate the pseudo-label noise and enhance the data diversity, we select 1 sample that is closest to the prototype in each selected cluster.
elements.sent-2411-211: We provide training and memory buffer updating details in Algorithm~\ref{algo:1 .}

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{DJAA for unsupervised lifelong person ReID} \label{algo:1} Input: Unlabeled domains $D_{1}\to ... \to D_{s} \to... \to D_{N}$, a hybrid memory buffer of size $n_{mem}$, an online encoder $\theta$ and a momentum encoder $\theta_{m}$. Output: Encoder $\theta_m$ after $N$-step adaptation. \begin{algorithmic}[1] \FOR {$D_{s}=D_{1}$ to $D_{N}$} \STATE Get $\theta_m$ from the last step $D_{s-1}$. Freeze a copy of momentum encoder $\theta_{s-1} \gets \theta_m$. Initialize the online encoder with the momentum encoder $\theta \gets \theta_m$; \FOR {$epoch=1$ to $E_{max}$} \STATE Generate pseudo labels on $D_s$; \STATE Calculate cluster prototypes $P$ in Eq.~(\ref{equa:cluster centroid}) on $D_s$; \FOR {$iter=1$ to $I_{max}$} \STATE Sample a mini-batch from the current domain $D_s$\ for $\mathcal{L}_{pa}$ in Eq.~(\ref{equa:cluster contrast}) and $\mathcal{L}_{ia}$ in Eq.~(\ref{equa:instance contrast}); \STATE Sample a mini-batch from the memory buffer for $\mathcal{L}_{ps}$ in Eq.~(\ref{equa:ps}) and $\mathcal{L}_{is}$ in Eq.~(\ref{equa:is}); \STATE Train $\theta$ with $\mathcal{L}_{overall}$ in Eq.~(\ref{equ:overall}); \STATE Update $\theta_m$ with Eq.~(\ref{equ:ema}); \ENDFOR \ENDFOR \STATE Update the memory buffer with $n_{new}$ new samples and $n_{old}$ previous samples, following Eq.~(\ref{equa:update number new}) and Eq.~(\ref{equa:update number old}); \STATE Store $\theta_m$ for next step $D_{s+1}$; \ENDFOR \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-214: Incremental training on seen datasets: We set 2 incremental training pipelines on seen datasets, \ie, one-cycle full set benchmark and two-cycle subset benchmark.
elements.sent-2411-215: The one-cycle full-set benchmark targets at evaluating the effectiveness of handling imbalanced class numbers between different adaptation steps, while the two-cycle subset benchmark aims to mimic the season and weather cycle, which may re-appear after several adaptation steps.
elements.sent-2411-216: In the one-cycle full set benchmark, we do not use any supervised pre-training.
elements.sent-2411-217: The model is directly adapted to Market1501, CUHK-SYSU, and MSMT17.
elements.sent-2411-218: We further define two training orders, \ie, Market$\to$Cuhk-Sysu$\to$MSMT17 and MSMT17$\to$Market$\to$Cuhk-Sysu.
elements.sent-2411-219: In the two-cycle subset benchmark, following CLUDA~\cite{huang2022lifelong , we first pre-train our model on PersonX in a supervised manner.}
elements.sent-2411-220: Then, we adapt our pre-trained model to Market1501, CUHK-SYSU, and MSMT17 in an unsupervised manner.
elements.sent-2411-221: The adaptation steps are repeated twice, with each stage involving a subset of 350 identities.
elements.sent-2411-222: The two-cycle training order is defined as PersonX$\to$Market $\to$Cuhk-Sysu$\to$MSMT17$\to$Market$\to$Cuhk-Sysu$\to$MSMT17.
elements.sent-2411-223: Cumulative Matching Characteristics (CMC) at Rank1 accuracy and mean Average Precision (mAP) are used in our experiments.
elements.sent-2411-224: We also report the averaged $\Bar{s Rank1$ and $\Bar{s mAP$ on the seen domains and unseen domains.}}
elements.sent-2411-225: Generalization ability on unseen datasets: We use 10 person ReID datasets to maximally evaluate the model generalization ability on different unseen domains, including VIPeR, PRID, GRID, iLIDS, CUHK01, CUHK02, SenseReID, CUHK03, 3DPeS and MMP-Retrieval.
elements.sent-2411-226: These 10 datasets cover all the unseen domains that are considered in previous supervised lifelong ReID methods~\cite{Wu2021GeneralisingWF,pu_cvpr2021 and domain generalizable ReID methods~\cite{Song2019GeneralizablePR .}}
elements.sent-2411-227: We use the traditional training/test split on CUHK03 dataset.
elements.sent-2411-228: Rank1 accuracy and mAP results are respectively reported on the test set of each unseen domain after the final step.
elements.sent-2411-229: Backward-compatible evaluation: To evaluate the backward compatibility of feature representations, we report retrieval performance between current query images and current gallery images (termed as self-test), and that between current query images and stored gallery images (termed as cross-test).
elements.sent-2411-230: Our method is implemented under Pytorch~\cite{PyTorch_NEURIPS2019 framework.}
elements.sent-2411-231: The total training time with 4 Nvidia 1080Ti GPUs is around 6 hours.
elements.sent-2411-232: We use an ImageNet \cite{Russakovsky2015ImageNetLS pre-trained ResNet50 \cite{he2016deep as our backbone network.}}
elements.sent-2411-233: For the strong data augmentation, we resize all images to 256$\times$128 and augment images with random horizontal flipping, cropping, Gaussian blurring and erasing \cite{Zhong2020RandomED .}
elements.sent-2411-234: For the weak data augmentation, we only resize images to 256$\times$128.
elements.sent-2411-235: At each step in the one-cycle full set training, we train our framework 30 epochs with 400 iterations per epoch using a Adam~\cite{Adam_optimization optimizer with a weight decay rate of 0.0005.}
elements.sent-2411-236: For the two-cycle subset evaluation, we follow CLUDA~\cite{huang2022lifelong to train our model for 60 epochs without specified iterations.}
elements.sent-2411-237: The learning rate is set to 0.00035 with a warm-up scheme in the first 10 epochs.
elements.sent-2411-238: No learning rate decay is used in the training.
elements.sent-2411-239: Pseudo labels on the current domain are updated on re-ranked Jaccard distance~\cite{zhong2017re at the beginning of each epoch with a DBSCAN~\cite{Ester1996ADA , in which the minimum cluster sample number is set to 4 and the distance threshold is set to 0.55.}}
elements.sent-2411-240: The momentum encoder is updated with a momentum hyper-parameter $\alpha=0.999$.
elements.sent-2411-241: To balance the model ability on old domains and the new domain, we separately take a mini-batch of current domain images and a mini-batch of buffered images of the same batch size $n_{bs}$, which is set to $n_{bs =32$ in our experiments.}
elements.sent-2411-242: Furthermore, we use a random identity sampler to construct mini-batches to handle the imbalanced images of different identities.
elements.sent-2411-243: Following the clustering setting on the current domain, the 32 current domain images are composed of 8 identities and 4 images per identity.
elements.sent-2411-244: Inside the mini-batch of buffered images, the 32 buffered images are composed of 32 identities and 1 image per identity.
elements.sent-2411-245: We use grid search to set the optimal temperature and balancing hyper-parameters in our proposed losses.
elements.sent-2411-246: Based on grid search results, we set the temperature hyper-parameters $\tau_{pa =0.5$, $\tau_{ia =0.1$, $\tau_{ps =0.1$ and $\tau_{is =0.2$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-247: To make rehearsal and adaptation losses on the same scale, we set the balancing hyper-parameters $\lambda_{ia =1$, $\lambda_{ps =10$ and $\lambda_{is =20$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-248: For the memory buffer, we set $n_{mem =512$, where the total identity number equals 512 and 1 image per cluster.}
elements.sent-2411-249: After the whole training, only the momentum encoder is saved for inference.

elements.sent-2411-329: Suitable data augmentation should be diverse but realistic, which means augmentation should not bring in distortions absent in the real dataset.
elements.sent-2411-330: The four augmentations (random flipping, cropping, blurring and erasing) are empirically selected.
elements.sent-2411-331: The results in Table~\ref{table:data augmentation ablation study show that adding color jitter augmentation achieves similar results.}
elements.sent-2411-332: Thus, we do not bother adding color jitter.
elements.sent-2411-333: In addition to regularizing the consistency between two encoders of different steps, we also introduce data augmentation perturbations to further enhance the model robustness.
elements.sent-2411-334: We report the performance of different data augmentation settings in Table~\ref{table:Augmentation consistency .}
elements.sent-2411-335: Different data augmentation settings on the prototype-level similarity consistency loss `$\mathcal{L _{ps}$' have only a slight influence on the final performance.}
elements.sent-2411-336: For the instance-level similarity consistency loss `$\mathcal{L _{is}$', the influence of the data augmentation setting is more evident.}
elements.sent-2411-337: We can observe that data augmentation brings in meaningful perturbations for consistency regularization.
elements.sent-2411-338: Using weakly augmented similarity as reference to regularize the strongly augmented prediction similarity with perturbations is the optimal setting for augmentation consistency regularization.

elements.sent-2411-347: As datasets are of different scales and diversity, it is meaningful to know whether the training order has a significant influence on the final results.
elements.sent-2411-348: Our primary training order starts from a medium domain Market and ends with the largest domain MSMT17.
elements.sent-2411-349: However, it is hard to control the order of upcoming domains in the real world.
elements.sent-2411-350: We test a second order MSMT17$\to$Market$\to$Cuhk-Sysu that starts from the largest domain MSMT17.
elements.sent-2411-351: In the second training order, it is easier to forget more knowledge on the largest domain MSMT17.
elements.sent-2411-352: Consequently, the performance under the training order \#2 is slightly inferior to that of the training order \#1.
elements.sent-2411-353: As shown in Table~\ref{table:order 2 seen , DJAA still significantly outperforms the state-of-the-art methods CLUDA and CVS on seen domains.}
elements.sent-2411-354: In the meantime, as shown in Table~\ref{table:order 2 unseen , DJAA also outperforms state-of-the-art methods on unseen domains.}
elements.sent-2411-355: We provide a forgetting curve on the seen domains in Fig.~\ref{fig:Non-forgetting evaluation order2 and a generalization ability on unseen domains in Fig.~\ref{fig:Generalizability evaluation order2 .}}
elements.sent-2411-356: Compared to previous methods, our proposed DJAA shows both strong anti-forgetting and generalization abilities.
elements.sent-2411-357: We further compare backward-compatible ability between state-of-the-art methods and our proposed method DJAA in the second training order.
elements.sent-2411-358: As shown in Table~\ref{table:order 2 seen , DJAA consistently shows superior backward-compatible ability over previous methods in the second training order.}

elements.subsubsection-2411-011: Data selection for memory buffer update
elements.sent-2411-359: To update the image memory at the end of each step, our strategy is to first select $n_{new}$ clusters with largest cluster sample numbers.
elements.sent-2411-360: The prototypes of selected clusters are used to update the memory buffer.
elements.sent-2411-361: Then, we select one image that is closest to its cluster prototype.
elements.sent-2411-362: In this way, we select the representative clusters for our image-to-prototype similarity consistency loss.
elements.sent-2411-363: As shown in Table~\ref{table:sample selection , we compare three possible memory buffer updating strategies.}
elements.sent-2411-364: We build a hybrid memory buffer to store cluster prototypes and image samples for the rehearsal module of our method DJAA.
elements.sent-2411-365: With a default memory size $n_{mem =512$, our image memory stores approximately $512$ images $\approx 0.8\%$ of all the training images (Market, Cuhk-Sysu and MSMT17).}
elements.sent-2411-366: Meanwhile, our prototype memory stores approximately $512$ prototype vectors (dimension $1\times2048\times1\times1$)$\approx$4.2 MB, which is negligible compared with storing dataset images (for example, MSMT17$\approx$2.5 GB).
elements.sent-2411-367: To further evaluate the dependency on the memory size, we vary the value of $n_{mem}$ and report the results in Table~\ref{table:n mem .}
elements.sent-2411-368: If the buffer size is limited to an extremely small number, such as 32 or 64, the results are even lower than our adaptation module baseline.
elements.sent-2411-369: When the buffer size is greater than or equals 128, the results are higher than the baseline.
elements.sent-2411-370: We can observe that the more data we store, the higher performance DJAA can achieve.
elements.sent-2411-371: As shown in Table~\ref{table:seen domains results , CLUDA~\cite{huang2022lifelong achieves 46.4\% mAP and 62.8\% mAP with a buffer size of 512.}}
elements.sent-2411-372: It is worth mentioning that our method achieves higher performance (49.8\% mAP and 67.3\% mAP) than CLUDA~\cite{huang2022lifelong , when the buffer size equals 128.}
elements.sent-2411-373: Backward compatible learning aims to maintain the consistency of representations after training more data, so that we do not need to re-extract gallery features after each adaptation step.
elements.sent-2411-374: As shown in Table~\ref{table:backward compatible order1 , with Market gallery features extracted at step 1, ICE has a cross-test performance 30.3\% mAP / 49.3\% Rank1 at step 2 and 3.4\% mAP / 6.3\% Rank1 at step 3.}
elements.sent-2411-375: In such case, Market gallery features need to be extracted at steps 1, 2 and 3, and Cuhk-Sysu gallery features needs to be extracted at steps 2 and 3.
elements.sent-2411-376: In contrast, reinforced by the feature back-compatibility, Market and Cuhk-Sysu gallery features need to be extracted only once in our method, at the 1$^{st}$ and 2$^{nd}$ step respectively.
elements.sent-2411-377: For example, with stored Market gallery features, DJAA has a cross-test performance 71.7\% mAP / 89.0\% Rank1 at step 2 and 65.2\% mAP / 85.6\% Rank1 at step 3.
elements.sent-2411-378: A real-world lifelong ReID system may involve more adaptation steps, where a backward compatible method can avoid repetitive gallery feature extraction and fasten the inference.
elements.sent-2411-379: In this paper, we propose an anti-forgetting adaptation method for unsupervised person ReID.
elements.sent-2411-380: The traditional unsupervised domain adaptation methods aim at obtaining the optimal performance on a fixed target domain, which shows poor anti-forgetting, generalization and backward-compatible ability.
elements.sent-2411-381: To tackle the three problems at once, we propose a Dual-level Joint Adaptation and Anti-forgetting (DJAA) method, which mainly consists of an adaptation module and a rehearsal module.
elements.sent-2411-382: In the adaptation module, we leverage a prototype-level contrastive loss and an instance-level contrastive loss to maximize the positive view similarity for learning new domain features.
elements.sent-2411-383: In the rehearsal module, our method regularizes the image-to-prototype and image-to-image similarity across domains to mitigate the knowledge forgetting.
elements.sent-2411-384: In comparison with previous lifelong methods, our proposed DJAA significantly improves the non-forgetting ability on seen domains and better generalization ability on unseen domains.. \ifCLASSOPTIONcaptionsoff \fi \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran.}
elements.sent-2411-385: DJAA supports multiple hardware platforms and already supports training and deployment on Ascend 910B NPUs.
